coredata.dc:title: Solvent-engineered carbon dots from rice straw for selective fluorescent detection of Cr(VI) and Pb(II)
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Jagpreet
Bhattu, Monika
Verma, Meenakshi
Brar, Satinder Kaur
Jadeja, Rajendrasinh
coredata.dc:description: Lead (Pb (II)) and hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) are recognized as major water pollutants, posing significant environmental and health concerns due to their toxicity and persistence. To address the critical need for their detection in aqueous systems, carbon dots (CDs) are prepared using rice straw as the raw material. The extraction was performed using two solvents of different polarity i.e., ethanol and hexane. Both the extracts were utilised for the CDs preparation viz CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH . Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis revealed that the average particle sizes of CDsHexane and CDsEtOH were 4.5 nm and 9.2 nm, respectively.FTIR analysis illustrates the presence of different kinds of functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl on CDs. The prepared CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH showed a fluorescent quenching response towards the detection of Cr(VI) and Pb(II). The quantitative analysis demonstrated a linear detection range of 1–10 µM for both contaminants. The detection limits were determined to be 0.19 µM and 10.94 µM for CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH , respectively. Further validation through real sample analysis confirmed the practical applicability of these CDs in detecting these contaminants in water, and the recovery was found to be above 90 %. Thus, the synthesized fluorescent probes have been proven an efficient platform for the fluorescent sensing of aforementioned target analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Chromium
Heavy metals
Lead
Fluorescence sensing
Rice straw

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–Vis and photoluminescence properties of CDs
pH stability and optimization of detection conditions
Quantum yield and stability of CDs towards target analytes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM images of CDshexane at 200 nm; b) TEM images of CDs EtOH at 50 nm; c) Particle size distribution histogram of CDs hexane illustrating the average size 5.45 nm; and d) Particle size distribution histogram of CDs EtOH illustrating the average size 9.2 nm.
FTIR spectra of CDs hexane (a) and CDs EtOH (b).
a) U.V. Visible absorption spectra of CDs hexane and CDs EtOH; and b) a) Fluorescence emissive spectra of CDs hexane and CDs EtOH .

a) Screening of photophysical behaviour of CDs hexane against toxic metals illustrating a fluorescent inhibition on addition of Cr(VI); b) Screening of photophysical behaviour of CDs EtOH against toxic metals illustrating a fluorescent inhibition on addition of Pb(II); c) Titration studies of CDs hexane for Cr(VI) (1 µM-10 µM); d) Titration studies of CDs EtOH by varying the concentration of Pb(II) (1 µM-10 µM).

a and b) Linear reductive response of CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH towards the varying concentration of Cr(VI) and Pb(II), respectively; c and d) Stern Volmer Plot of CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH towards the detection of Cr(VI) and Pb(II), respectively.

a and b) Effect of pH on CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH towards Cr(VI) and Pb(II), respectively; c and d) Interference studies against other toxic metals for CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH towards Cr(VI) and Pb(II), respectively.
Stability of the CD hexane and CD EtOH and their corresponding complexes with Cr(VI) and Pb(II), respectively.
Comparative analysis of carbon dots towards the detection of Cr(VI) and Pb(II).

head.ce:title: Solvent-engineered carbon dots from rice straw for selective fluorescent detection of Cr(VI) and Pb(II)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil Engineering, Lassonde School of Engineering, York University, Toronto, ON M3J 1P3, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eDepartment of Civil Engineering, Lassonde School of Engineering, York University, Toronto, ON M3J 1P3, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lead (Pb (II)) and hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) are recognized as major water pollutants, posing significant environmental and health concerns due to their toxicity and persistence. To address the critical need for their detection in aqueous systems, carbon dots (CDs) are prepared using rice straw as the raw material. The extraction was performed using two solvents of different polarity i.e., ethanol and hexane. Both the extracts were utilised for the CDs preparation viz CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH . Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis revealed that the average particle sizes of CDsHexane and CDsEtOH were 4.5 nm and 9.2 nm, respectively.FTIR analysis illustrates the presence of different kinds of functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl on CDs. The prepared CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH showed a fluorescent quenching response towards the detection of Cr(VI) and Pb(II). The quantitative analysis demonstrated a linear detection range of 1–10 µM for both contaminants. The detection limits were determined to be 0.19 µM and 10.94 µM for CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH , respectively. Further validation through real sample analysis confirmed the practical applicability of these CDs in detecting these contaminants in water, and the recovery was found to be above 90 %. Thus, the synthesized fluorescent probes have been proven an efficient platform for the fluorescent sensing of aforementioned target analytes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pb(II) and Cr(VI) are highly poisonous heavy metals widely spread in aquatic ecosystems. The contamination is significantly caused due to industrial processes such leather tanning, electroplating, mining, wood preservation, metal polishing, printing, and the creation of pigments and polymers [1]. Unlike its counterpart Cr(III), which is biologically essential for protein and glucose metabolism, Cr(VI) is recognized for its potent toxicity and ability to easily diffuse through cellular membranes due to its strong oxidative nature [2]. Cr(VI) bioaccumulates in living organisms even at low concentrations which leads to serious health problems as skin ulcers, pulmonary sensitivity, dental erosion, renal damage, respiratory ailments, liver and kidney damage, genetic abnormalities, and in the worst situations, death [3,4]. Regulatory agencies like the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) and the World Health Organization (WHO) have set stringent permissible limits for Cr(VI) in drinking water at 0.1 mg L⁻¹ (1.92 μM) and 0.05 mg L⁻¹ (0.96 μM), respectively [5,6]. Secondly, Pb(II), the heaviest stable element, is highly toxic even at trace levels and poses significant health risks, including respiratory diseases, cognitive impairments, anaemia, hypertension, kidney damage, and neurological disorders [7–9]. Regulatory bodies like the European Parliament and the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have implemented restrictions, such as limiting Pb (II), in electronics and setting a permissible level of 15 ng mL⁻¹ in drinking water [10]. Despite these measures, Pb (II) persists in the environment through sources like leaded gasoline, cigarette smoke, and the corrosion of lead pipes, contaminating water and soil and posing ongoing threats to human health and ecosystems [11,12].

Hence, the detection of Cr(VI) and Pb(II) in environmental samples is crucial due to its classification as a widespread industrial pollutant with serious health risks. The measurement of Cr(VI) and Pb(II) at micromolar levels is necessary to comply with international water quality standards, requiring analytical methods with high sensitivity to detect even trace levels of the contaminant. Additionally, these methods must demonstrate excellent selectivity to differentiate Cr(VI) and Pb(II) from coexisting ions such as Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, and relatively non-toxic Cr(III), as well as other pollutants like As(V). Such precision ensures the accurate assessment of industrial waste impact and drinking water quality, contributing to effective environmental and public health protection measures [12,13].

Till now, numerous analytical techniques were introduced for sensing Cr(VI) and Pb(II) in various matrices. Common approaches include chromatography [14,15], spectrophotometry [16,17], spectrofluorimetric, mass spectrometry [18,19], electrochemistry [20,21] and atomic absorption spectrometry [22–24]. Among these, spectrofluorimetric utilizing Cr(VI) and Pb(II) selective fluorescent probes has gained particular interest due to its simplicity, rapid response, and high sensitivity [13,25].While advanced methods such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [26], electrochemical techniques [27], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [28,29], and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [18,30] offer precise quantification of Cr(VI) and Pb(II) ions, they often require sophisticated instrumentation, are costly, and are less suitable for on-site analysis. Additionally, many of these techniques struggle to differentiate between chromium species, providing only the cumulative chromium content rather than distinguishing between Cr(VI) and Cr(III) [31]. In recent years, optical sensors pops up due to their rapid response, affordability, efficiency, real-time monitoring capability, and portability. Fluorescence-based chemosensors, a subset of optical sensors, are particularly effective for detecting analytes through fluorescence enhancement or quenching [32,33]. Furthermore, the current methods holds advantages for its non-enzymatic activities as the immunoassay/ enzymatic sensors hold less thermal stability and shelf life [34]. These sensors have garnered significant attention for Cr(VI) and Pb(II) sensing owing to their ability to provide reliable and specific results. A wide array of fluorescent probes has been reported for detecting heavy metals, with special focus on Cr(VI) and Pb(II) ions [35,36]. To ensure their suitability for sensor applications, these probes must meet several critical criteria. They should exhibit a substantial change in fluorescence intensity upon interaction with the target analyte, such as fluorescence quenching or enhancement. Additionally, high fluorescence quantum yields, large Stokes shifts, notable extinction coefficients, and robust photostability are essential for their practical application [37,38].

Taking the advantages of fluorescent probes into consideration, carbon dots have been well recognised for their efficient luminescent and sensing behaviour [39,40]. Hence, in current study, CDs have been synthesized using the waste material which basically cease the two major environmental problems i.e., environmental pollution as well as waste management. As rice straw is considered as the majorly generated waste worldwide specifically in India [41,42]. Thus, the rice straw is employed for preparing CDs complies with waste management issue and also fulfils the criteria of circular economy as well as sustainable development goals (SDGs).

Thus, the current research involves the synthesis of two kinds of CDs based on the extraction using solvents of different polarity. The synthesized CDs were further be explored for detecting Cr(VI) and Pb(II). Further, the photophysical studies be conducted in order to find the selectivity and sensitivity of sensor. The current study suggests that the choice of solvent used in the extraction and synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) plays a crucial role in determining their structural and physicochemical properties, particularly their surface functionalities. Different solvents influence the types of functional groups present on the CD surface, altering their charge distribution, hydrophilicity, and interaction potential with metal ions. This variation in surface chemistry directly affects the selective detection capabilities of CDs, enabling them to target specific metal ions based on their affinity for particular functional groups. Furthermore, the varying interaction of solvent tuned CDs with target analytes paves a way for the future researchers to introduce different degradation mechanisms for the pollutants

Chemicals were procured from Shimadzu and Merck) and used as such. Distilled water was used as the solvent throughout experiment. A Cr(VI)and Pb(II) stock solution (100 mM) was prepared in 5 mL of deionized water. Further dilutions of the stock solution to 1 mM and 100 mM were made as needed. Other metal ions’ solutions were prepared from their corresponding nitrate salts.

To prepare rice straw-derived carbon dots (R-CDs), the rice straw was initially washed thoroughly with tap water to remove any surface contaminants. Subsequently, it was rinsed with distilled water to ensure further purification. The cleaned straw was then dried in a hot air oven at 100 °C for 12 h to eliminate any remaining moisture. Once dried, the straw was ground into a fine powder. A 5 g portion of this powdered material was subjected to Soxhlet extraction, using ethanol and hexane as solvents for durations of 8 and 6 h, respectively. This extended extraction process was employed to ensure the efficient recovery of phytochemicals present in the biomass, such as phenolic compounds, organic acids, and flavonoids. Notably, the ethanolic extract primarily contained flavonoids, phenolics, and alkaloids[43]. In contrast, hexane being the non-polar solvent mostly extract lipophilic components including fatty acids, terpenoids, and other hydrocarbons [43,44]. These phytochemicals contribute significantly to the incorporation of functional groups, such as hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH), and carbonyl (-C = O), onto the surface of the carbon dots. The presence of these functional groups improves the surface reactivity of the carbon dots, facilitating efficient interactions with specific contaminants. These interactions occur through various mechanisms, including electrostatic attraction, hydrogen bonding, and coordination with metal ions. The extracts obtained from Soxhlet extraction was filtered to remove solid residues, and 30 mL of each filtrate was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was subjected to hydrothermal treatment in a muffle furnace under varying conditions of temperature and time, including 180 °C for durations of 6 h, 12 h, and 18 h, to optimize the synthesis parameters for R-CDs. The resultant solution was cooled and filtered. The supernatants were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm with three consecutive washings (Fig. 1 ).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained using a JEOL transmission electron microscope operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using Perkin Elmer spectrophotometer, covering the range of 4000 to 500 cm⁻¹. Fluorescence emission spectra were measured on a Shimadzu fluorescence spectrophotometer, and UV–vis absorption spectra were captured using a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer. The crystalline structure of the carbon dots was examined through X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, performed on a Bruker AXS D8 Focus diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54056 Å).

Fluorescence titrations with Cr(VI) and Pb(II) were conducted by adding specific aliquots of metal ion solutions solution to 2 mL of CD solution (in a cuvette. Cr(VI) and Pb(II) was added stepwise using a microinjector, ensuring negligible changes in the solution volume. The mixture was incubated for 1 min, followed by fluorescence excitation at 350 and 270 nm for ethanol and hexane CDs, respectively, and the fluorescence was recorded for each titration step. The real samples analysis was performed by spiking the different samples with known concentration of metal ions and the recovery analysis was performed.

The surface morphology and structural characteristics of CDs were examined using TEM. The samples were prepared using a standardized procedure to ensure reproducibility. Initially, the synthesized CDs were dispersed in ultrapure water at a concentration of 1 mg/mL and subjected to 10–15 min of sonication to achieve uniform dispersion and minimize aggregation. A 200-mesh copper grid coated with an ultra-thin carbon film was selected for sample deposition. Prior to use, the grid underwent plasma treatment for 1–2 min to enhance sample adhesion. Subsequently, 5–10 µL of the well-dispersed CD solution was carefully drop-cast onto the grid and allowed to dry at ambient temperature in a clean environment. To further eliminate any residual moisture, the grid was placed in a vacuum desiccator for 30–60 min. For long-term stability, the prepared grid was stored in a dry nitrogen atmosphere to prevent oxidation or structural changes. The shape of the CDs EtOH was observed to be completely spherical (Fig. 2a ), whereas the CDs heaxne showed the distorted structure (Fig. 2b). The TEM images revealed that the CDs have a size distribution ranging from 4.9 to 5.9 nm, and 8.5 to 9.9 nm demonstrating an average size of approximately 5.45 nm and 9.2 nm for CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH , respectively (Fig. 2c, d).

FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to demonstrate the surface functionalities present on both kinds of CDs. The FTIR spectra of CDs heaxne revealed several characteristic peaks i.e., at 1054 cm−1; 1242 cm−1; 1375 cm−1 and 1742 cm−1 which are attributed towards the presence of C–O stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of alcohol or ether groups; C–O stretching in esters or carboxylic acids; C–H bending vibrations, commonly associated with aliphatic hydrocarbons or methyl groups; and C = O stretching vibrations, typically observed in carbonyl groups such as ketones, aldehydes, or esters, respectively. These functional groups imply that the CDs possess oxygen-containing groups, which enhance their hydrophilicity (Fig. 3a ). On the other hand, the CDs EtOH exhibited broad absorption bands in the range of 3000–3500 cm⁻¹ which are associated with the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H bonds. The characteristic peaks observed at 1433 cm⁻¹ and 1545 cm⁻¹ are indicative of C = C stretching vibrations. Furthermore, the absorption bands at 1833 cm⁻¹ and 2079 cm⁻¹ correspond to C = O stretching and CC or CN vibrations, respectively. These findings suggest the presence of abundant amino functionalities on the surface of the CDs. The combination of these functional groups enhances the hydrophilicity and stability of the CDs, making them highly effective for use as fluorescent probes (Fig. 3b) [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis and photoluminescence properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis and photoluminescence (PL) analysis of the CDs were analysed under varying excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4a ). The UV–Vis spectrum of CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH shows two prominent absorption bands: one at 226 nm and 224 nm, respectively which is attributed to the presence of multiple polyaromatic chromophores, and another at 278 nm and 274 nm, respectively which are associated to the n–π* transition. The PL spectrum demonstrates that the emission wavelength of CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH are largely excitation-independent when excited at wavelengths below 400 nm. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths for CDs heaxne were observed at 270 nm and 340 nm, respectively, while for CDs EtOH , these wavelengths were recorded at 270 nm and 460 nm (Fig. 4b).

To evaluate the sensing potential of synthesized CDs for metal ions, photoluminescence analysis was conducted. Initially, the synthesized CDs (both CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH ) were tested for various toxic metal ions including Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺, Ni²⁺, Co²⁺, Zn²⁺, Cd²⁺, Hg²⁺, Mn²⁺, Cu²⁺, K⁺, and Pb²⁺. For this assessment, 100 µL of 1 mM solutions (100 µM) of each metal ion were mixed with 5 mL of synthesized CDs the fluorescence response was monitored. Among the tested ions, CDs hexane demonstrated a significant "turn-off" fluorescence behaviour towards Cr(VI) (Fig. 5a ); whereas CDs EtOH showed a fluorescent quenched effect in case of Pb(II) indicating a strong interaction between the CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH and Cr(VI) and Pb(II) ions, respectively (Fig. 5a, b).

Subsequently, titration studies were performed to determine the sensitivity and binding affinity of CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH and Cr(VI) and Pb(II) ions, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5c, the sequential addition of Cr(VI) ions (10 µL to 500 µL) resulted in a gradual increase in the fluorescence intensity of CDs heaxne . Similar response was observed in case of CDs EtOH (Fig. 5d).

Moreover, a linear curve has been plotted by taking the concentration of target analytes (Cr(VI) and Pb(II)) as x-axis and the fluorescence response at emission maxima of CDs as y axis. The synthesized CDs exhibited a linear fluorescence response within a concentration range of 1–100 µM (Fig. 6a and b). Additionally, Stern Volmer plot was constructed by plotting the normalized fluorescence intensity against the concentrations of Cr(VI) and Pb(II) concentration for CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH , respectively. (Fig. 6c and d). The quenching constant was calculated using the S-V plot and the detection limit (LOD) was estimated using 3 sigma method:
L O D =
3.3 * S D
S l o p e
where SD represents the standard deviation of the blank measurements, and Slope denotes the slope of the calibration curve or SV plot. The calculated LOD for Cr(VI) and Pb(II) was 19.19 µM and 10.94 µM respectively. The SV constant was found to be 6.67 × 104 M−1 and 0.48 × 104 M−1 for CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH, respectively which suggests a strong binding of synthesized CDs with the target analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH stability and optimization of detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of pH on CDs' fluorescence was investigated over a pH range of 2–14 (Fig. 7). The results demonstrated minimal variation in fluorescence intensity across this range, indicating that the CDs are stable under diverse pH conditions. For Cr(VI) detection, a solution pH of ∼6 was chosen, where HCrO₄⁻ and CrO₄²⁻ coexist. The CDs demonstrated high resistance to photobleaching. The CDs were found to be stable over a temperature range of 25 °C to 60 °C.

Further, to assess the selectivity of sensing of Cr(VI) and Pb(II), the interference effects of coexisting ions were evaluated under optimized conditions. In this regard, 100 µL of all the ions were mixed to the 5 mL solution of CDs hexane + Cr(VI) and CDs EtOH + Pb(II). Most ions did not interfere significantly at concentrations >100-fold higher than Cr(VI), while Cu²⁺ and Fe³⁺ showed mild interference at concentrations ≤10-fold and ≤5-fold, respectively (Fig. 7 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield and stability of CDs towards target analytes
ce:section.ce:para:
The relative quantum yield (ΦS) of the photoluminescence (PL) emission was determined by comparing the fluorescence response of the sample with that of a reference fluorophore (Quinine sulphate for CD EtOH and coumarin for CD hexane ) with a known quantum yield. The integral photoluminescence intensities of the sample (PLS) and reference (PLref) were recorded under identical experimental conditions [34].
Φ
S
=
Φ
r e f
(
P
L S
P
L
r e f
)
*
(
A
r e f
A S
)
*
(
n S 2
n
r e f
2
)

The absorbance values at the excitation wavelength for the sample (AS) and reference (A ref) were also measured to account for differences in light absorption. Additionally, the refractive indices of the respective solvents (nS for the sample and nref for the reference) were considered to correct for variations in optical density and scattering. All measurements were performed under the same instrumental geometry to ensure consistency and reliability of the quantum yield calculation. ΦS is the quantum yield of the reference which is 0.54 in case of quinine sulphate and 0.58 in case of coumarin. Using the above equation the quantum yield of CDs heaxne and CDs EtOH was calculated to be 0.48 and 0.4, respectively. Furthermore, the stability of CDs was conducted over a period of 60 days in which the fluorescence response of CDs and the target analyte was checked after every 15 days. No significant change has been observed in the sensing response CDs towards the sensing of analyte after 60 days (Fig. 8 ). Additionally, to evaluate the reproducibility and consistency of the synthesized CDs, the CDs were synthesized under identical conditions. Characterization using UV–Visible absorption, fluorescence spectroscopy, and TEM confirmed that the optical and structural properties remained consistent. The fluorescence emission exhibited a slight variation of ±4 nm, while the quantum yield differences between batches remained within ±2 %, indicating high reproducibility. These findings confirm that the synthesis process reliably produces CDs with uniform properties. Various studies have already been reported in literature which illustrates the comparison of the waste derived CDs for the recognition of Cr(VI) and Pb(II) (Table 1 ).

The selective response of CDs heaxne towards Cr(VI) and CDs EtOH towards Pb(II) is primarily attributed to differences in their surface chemistry, solvent polarity effects, and the specific interactions governing their fluorescence quenching mechanisms. The choice of solvent during carbon dot (CD) synthesis significantly influences their structural and functional properties, leading to variations in metal ion recognition. Surface chemistry plays a crucial role in determining the selectivity of CDs for different metal ions. CDs synthesized in a non-polar solvent like hexane tend to have hydrophobic surfaces with minimal oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxyl (-COOH). Instead, their surface is dominated by weakly polar moieties, including CH and C = C bonds. This structural composition makes CDHexane highly responsive to Cr(VI), which primarily exists in solution as chromate (CrO₄²⁻) or dichromate (Cr₂O₇²⁻). The interaction between CD_Hexane and Cr(VI) is primarily driven by electrostatic attraction and π-π stacking interactions. Furthermore, Cr(VI) exhibits a broad absorption spectrum that significantly overlaps with the excitation and emission spectra of CDs hexane , leading to fluorescence quenching through the inner filter effect (IFE) [54]. On the other hand, CDs EtOH synthesized in ethanol—a polar protic solvent—exhibits a high density of hydrophilic functional groups such as hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH), and amino (-NH₂). These groups strongly coordinate with Pb(II), leading to selective fluorescence quenching through static quenching and electron transfer mechanisms [55]. The ability of Pb(II) to form stable complexes with oxygen- and nitrogen-containing moieties on CDs EtOH enhances its selectivity towards lead ions, whereas Cr(VI) does not exhibit the same degree of interaction with these functional groups. The influence of solvent polarity further explains this selectivity. Since hexane is a low-polarity solvent, the CDs synthesized in it exhibit lower surface oxidation and greater hydrophobicity, which favors interactions with oxyanions like Cr(VI). In contrast, ethanol, being a moderately polar solvent, induces higher surface oxidation, making CDs EtOH more hydrophilic and capable of coordinating with Pb(II) through complexation mechanisms [55].

The analysis of real water samples was performed using different water sources, including river water, tap water, and reverse osmosis (RO) water. All the samples were spiked with known concentration of target analytes viz below the detection limit, at detection limit and two concentrations above the detection limit. Thus, a total of 24 samples (12 for Cr(VI) and 12 for Pb(II)) were analysed for the recovery, and the recovery percentages were determined to assess the accuracy and reliability of the detection method. The spiked samples below the detection limit didn't show any presence of target anaytes. The recovery percentage for both the probes was found to be above 90 %.

To summarize, this study presents the demonstration of a facile and environmentally friendly approach for highly luminescent CDs mediated by rice straw. The CDs were prepared by extracting the phytoconstituents from rice straw using the solvents of different polarity, i.e., ethanol and hexane. The CDs hexane and CDs EtOH were prepared hydrothermally based on the polarity of extract. These CDs hexane and CDs EtOH exhibit exceptional potential for Cr(VI) and Pb(II) sensing in water samples. The synthesized CDs hexane and CDs EtOH display an average particle size in the range of 3–15 nm and emit strong photoluminescence in the blue region. They demonstrate excellent fluorescence stability across various physical conditions, and remarkable photostability. The surface of the CDs predominantly contains carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups, facilitating efficient metal ion binding. These CDs hexane and CDs EtOH exhibit a detection limit (LOD) of 19.19 µM and 10.94 µM for Cr(VI) and Pb(II), respectively with no incubation time required. Validation experiments confirm their effectiveness in detecting Cr(VI) and Pb(II) in both deionized (DI) water and metal ion-contaminated tap water. Thus, the synthesized fluorescent probes have been proven a highly efficient platform for the water decontamination. Further, future research should explore their potential role in advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), electrocatalysis, and synergistic adsorption-catalysis systems to enhance water purification efficiency. Moreover, in industrial perspective, further optimization is required to enhance scalability, cost-effectiveness, and long-term stability of these CDs for large-scale deployment in real-world environmental monitoring and remediation. The key research demands include improving synthesis yield, integrating CDs into smart sensing platforms (e.g., microfluidic devices), and exploring their potential for multi-contaminant detection. Addressing these aspects will facilitate the commercial translation of CD-based fluorescent sensors into industrially viable water treatment solutions.

Jagpreet Singh: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Monika Bhattu: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Meenakshi Verma: Validation, Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Satinder Kaur Brar: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Rajendrasinh Jadeja: Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chromium speciation in rainwater: temporal variability and atmospheric deposition
Environ. Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.J. Kieber, J.D. Willey, S.D. Zvalaren, Chromium speciation in rainwater: temporal variability and atmospheric deposition, Environ. Sci. Technol.. 36 (2002) 5321–5327.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The inner filter effect of Cr (VI) on Tb-doped layered rare earth hydroxychlorides: new fluorescent adsorbents for the simple detection of Cr (VI)
Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Kim, B.-I. Lee, S.-H. Byeon, The inner filter effect of Cr (VI) on Tb-doped layered rare earth hydroxychlorides: new fluorescent adsorbents for the simple detection of Cr (VI), Chem. Commun.. 51 (2015) 725–728.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Methacrylate copolymers with pendant piperazinedione-sensing motifs as fluorescent chemosensory materials for the detection of Cr (VI) in aqueous media
J. Hazard. Mater.
